title: Before You Unleash the AI, De-Complect the Problem
tldr: AI makes feature soup cheap. Before asking an agent to build, run a quick de-complect review: name the concepts, split only the ones that vary independently, and demand proof that any extra moving part helps.
description: A practical, public-safe Continuum Kit note on using Rich Hickey's simple-not-easy lens before AI-assisted implementation.
audience: Smart outsiders who build or manage software with AI help, and can feel the pain of feature sprawl without needing private Continuum or Commandbook context.
takeaway: AI makes it dangerously easy to add more stuff. The useful skill is keeping the product simple by untangling concepts before building.
evidence: ChatGPT seed article about the Hickeyian way.
Complecting as tying together concerns that should vary independently.
Clojure reusable functions over generic data and the Seq abstraction.
examples: A note-taking app that turns into a task manager, calendar, CRM, and dashboard.
A settings object that absorbs permissions, UI state, billing, feature flags, and preferences.
An AI-generated prototype with ten plausible buttons but no obvious core action.
counterpoints: Separating things is a guideline, not a religion.
Datomic's separately deployable pieces can still create operational pain.
mood: Clear, warm, slightly wry, practical-philosophical, and not an architecture lecture.
sensitivity: No private agent or repo internals.
No claim that internal security or publishing systems are production-ready.
callToAction: Next project you start, after grilling but before you set the AI off implementing, try the de-complect review skill.
